summary: This page continues the introduction to complex numbers by explaining the geometric meaning of the complex conjugate and showing that a number and its conjugate sum to twice the real part and multiply to the squared magnitude. It then uses the polar form $re^{j\theta}$ and a figure-based interpretation to derive useful exponential identities such as $e^{\pm jn\pi}=-1$ for odd integers, $e^{\pm j2n\pi}=1$, and $e^{\pm j\pi/2}=\pm j$.

figures.caption: Two diagrams illustrating complex numbers. (a) shows a complex number re^jθ represented as a vector in the complex plane with real (Re) and imaginary (Im) axes. (b) shows the unit circle in the complex plane with points 1, -1, j, and -j marked, along with their corresponding angles π, -π, π/2, and -π/2.
